Where Does Money Come From?

10 min read · book · 2026-03-02
moneybankingmonetary-systemcreditcentral-bankingmacroeconomicsfinancial-reform
Source: https://new-economicsf.files.svdcdn.com/production/files/b847162e8c996d5e26_fam6...

Summary

El libro derriba el mito más persistente de la economía mainstream: que los bancos son simples intermediarios que prestan el dinero de los ahorradores. La tesis central es que el 97% del dinero en circulación no lo crea el banco central — lo crean los bancos comerciales privados cada vez que emiten un crédito. Cuando un banco presta, no transfiere dinero existente: crea simultáneamente un activo (el préstamo) y un pasivo (el depósito) de la nada. El dinero es, fundamentalmente, una relación social de crédito y deuda, no una mercancía.

Esto tiene implicaciones brutales para la regulación. Los requerimientos de capital (Basel II/III) no limitan la creación de crédito — solo actúan post-facto. Y el banco central no controla la cantidad de reservas que tienen los bancos; son los bancos comerciales los que determinan cuántas reservas necesitan y el banco central las provee para mantener el sistema funcionando. El modelo del "multiplicador monetario" que se enseña en universidades es, en palabras de los autores, incompleto y engañoso.

La consecuencia más importante: los bancos deciden dónde va el crédito nuevo. Y los incentivos los empujan sistemáticamente hacia activos existentes (bienes raíces, activos financieros) en lugar de inversión productiva. Esto explica burbujas de activos, estancamiento industrial y crisis financieras recurrentes — no como anomalías, sino como consecuencias estructurales del diseño del sistema.

Key Takeaways

  • Los bancos comerciales crean el 97% del dinero mediante extensión de crédito — no son intermediarios
  • La creación de un préstamo genera simultáneamente el depósito: el dinero sale de la nada
  • El banco central no controla la oferta monetaria directamente; fija tasas de interés y provee las reservas que los bancos demandan
  • El modelo del multiplicador monetario (enseñado en todas las universidades) es falso en la práctica moderna
  • Capital adequacy requirements (Basilea) no previenen burbujas de crédito — no limitan la creación de dinero
  • El crédito se raciona por confianza, no por tasas de interés; en crisis, el canal monetario se rompe
  • Los bancos prefieren prestar contra colateral (inmuebles, activos) vs. inversión productiva — diseño que genera desigualdad estructural
  • La política fiscal del gobierno (déficit, deuda) no expande directamente la oferta monetaria
  • El Quantitative Easing inunda de reservas el sistema, pero esas reservas no circulan en la economía real
  • El dinero como mercancía (teoría clásica/neoclásica) es históricamente incorrecto — la evidencia muestra que el crédito precede al dinero-mercancía

Full Text / Transcript

JOSH RYAN-COLLINS, TONY GREENHAM, RICHARD WERNER, ANDREW JACKSON FOREWORD BY CHARLES A.E. GOODHART

WHERE DOES MONEY COME FROM? A guide to the UK monetary and banking system

Published by nef (the new economics foundation), 2nd edition 2012. Licensed under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0.

---

Foreword (Charles Goodhart, LSE)

Far from money being 'the root of all evil', our economic system cannot cope without it. Hence the shock-horror when the Lehman failure raised the spectre of an implosion of our banking system.

The supply of money is actually determined primarily by the demand of borrowers to take out bank loans. When such demand is low (weak economy, zero interest rates), the relationship between central bank reserves and commercial bank lending breaks down entirely. Flooding banks with liquidity via QE has not led to commensurate increases in bank lending or broad money.

---

Chapter 1: Introduction

Key findings:

The money supply and how it is created

New money is principally created by commercial banks when they extend or create credit — making loans, overdrafts, or buying existing assets. In creating credit, banks simultaneously create brand new deposits in borrowers' accounts.

UK money exists in three forms: 1. Cash (banknotes and coins) 2. Central bank reserves (held by commercial banks at the Bank of England) 3. Commercial bank money (bank deposits created when banks extend credit)

Physical cash = less than 3% of total circulating money. Commercial bank money = 97%.

Popular misconceptions of banking

The simple version (banks take deposits and lend them out) is wrong. Banks do not need to wait for a customer to deposit money before making a new loan. The making of a loan creates a new deposit.

Fractional reserve banking is more accurate but still misleading — it still treats banks as mere intermediaries. At the time of the financial crisis, banks held just £1.25 in reserves for every £100 issued as credit.

The commercial banks determine the quantity of central bank reserves the Bank of England must lend to them — not the other way around.

Implications of commercial bank money creation

1. Capital adequacy requirements do not constrain money creation — they don't prevent credit booms or asset bubbles 2. Credit is rationed by confidence (that the loan will be repaid, that the system is solvent), not by interest rates 3. Banks allocate credit toward collateral/existing assets rather than productive investment → money flows into property and financial speculation, not SMEs and manufacturing 4. Fiscal policy does not in itself expand the money supply — government has no direct involvement in money creation

---

Chapter 2: What Do Banks Do?

Three forms of money: Cash, central bank reserves, commercial bank money.

How banks create money by extending credit: When a bank makes a loan of £X, it simultaneously:

  • Creates an asset (the loan) on its balance sheet
  • Creates a liability (the new deposit in the borrower's account)
No existing money is transferred. New purchasing power is created.

The textbook multiplier model and its problems: The textbook model says: central bank creates base money → banks lend multiples of it → money multiplies through the system. This implies the central bank controls money supply by controlling reserves.

In practice: banks extend credit based on demand and profitability. They then seek the reserves they need. The central bank accommodates their reserve needs to maintain system stability. The causality runs backwards from the textbook.

How money is actually created: Banks operate within an electronic clearing system that nets out multilateral payments daily. They need only a tiny fraction of central bank money to meet payment requirements.

---

Chapter 3: The Nature and History of Money and Banking

Two theories of money:

1. Commodity theory (orthodox): Money is a neutral medium of exchange that evolved from barter. Money is a veil over real economic activity. Classical and neoclassical economics.

2. Credit theory (heterodox, supported by the authors): Money is a social relationship — an IOU backed by trust and state power. Credit predates commodity money historically. Anthropological and historical evidence supports this.

Historical evidence for credit theory:

  • Ancient Mesopotamian clay tablets record debts (credit) before commodity money existed
  • The state defines what counts as money (legal tender, tax payment)
  • Modern money is entirely credit-based — no commodity backing since 1971 (Bretton Woods collapse)
Key historical developments:
  • Promissory notes → goldsmiths discovered they could issue more notes than gold held → fractional reserve banking born
  • Bank of England created 1694 via bond issuance — private institution lending to the Crown
  • Gold Standard imposed discipline but caused deflation and crises
  • WWI: gold standard suspended; government took control of credit
  • 1970s-80s: deregulation of banking, rise of wholesale money markets, explosion of credit
  • Digital money: 99% of money now electronic
---

Chapter 4: Money and Banking Today

What counts as money: Defined by liquidity and acceptability. A spectrum from cash (most liquid) to illiquid assets.

Banks as money creators: T-chart example:

  • Bank creates loan: Asset: Loan £100 / Liability: Deposit £100
  • No prior deposit required
Payment system:
  • Commercial banks hold reserve accounts at the Bank of England
  • Interbank payments settle through these reserves (RTGS system)
  • Net multilateral clearing at day's end minimizes reserve needs
  • Banks need very few reserves relative to deposits outstanding
Cash and seigniorage:
  • Government earns seigniorage (profit from issuing cash)
  • Cash is ~3% of money supply
  • Cash is a form of government debt (Bank of England liability)
Quantitative Easing:
  • Central bank creates new reserves → buys government bonds from pension funds/banks
  • New reserves go to commercial banks' accounts at BoE
  • Banks don't automatically lend these out — QE inflates asset prices but has limited effect on real economy lending
  • "Pushing on a string" when loan demand is weak
Solvency and capital:
  • Banks must maintain capital (equity) as buffer against losses
  • Capital requirements (Basel) don't prevent money creation — they're a solvency backstop, not a money supply control
---

Chapter 5: Regulating Money Creation and Allocation

Capital adequacy rules (Basel):

  • Banks must hold capital proportional to risk-weighted assets
  • But this does not limit credit creation — banks can always raise capital after extending credit
  • Risk weighting incentivizes lending against safe collateral (mortgages) over productive lending
Liquidity regulation:
  • Compulsory reserve ratios (UK abolished these in 1981)
  • Sterling stock liquidity regime — requires minimum liquid assets
Shadow banking and the financial crisis:
  • Securitization allowed banks to move loans off balance sheets → obscured true leverage
  • Shadow banking system (hedge funds, money market funds, SIVs) grew massively
  • 2008 crisis: solvency crisis (assets worth less than liabilities) + liquidity crisis (no interbank lending)
  • Regulatory frameworks failed completely
Endogenous vs. exogenous money:
  • Exogenous (orthodox view): central bank controls money supply
  • Endogenous (what actually happens): money supply is demand-driven; banks create credit as demanded; central bank accommodates
Quantity Theory of Credit (Werner):
  • GDP growth correlates with credit creation for real-economy purposes
  • Credit for asset purchases → asset bubbles, not GDP growth
  • Credit composition matters, not just quantity
International examples of direct credit regulation:
  • Japan (postwar): "window guidance" — BoJ directly instructed banks how much credit to allocate by sector
  • Germany: relationship banking, Sparkassen (savings banks) focused on SME lending
  • Controls on credit allocation can redirect money into productive investment
---

Chapter 6: Government Finance and Foreign Exchange

EU constraints:

  • Maastricht Treaty prohibits direct monetary financing of government
  • ECB cannot directly fund member state governments
  • Eurozone crisis: structural inability of peripheral states to monetize debt → forced austerity
Fiscal policy mechanics:
  • Government taxes → money flows from private sector to government account at BoE
  • Government spends → money flows back to private sector
  • Net effect on money supply depends on balance of taxation and spending
  • Government borrowing (issuing gilts) ≠ money creation: gilts are exchanged for existing bank reserves
"Crowding out" myth:
  • Orthodox view: government borrowing crowds out private investment by competing for limited savings
  • Reality: banks create money for lending; there's no fixed pot of savings
  • But government borrowing can affect interest rates and credit allocation
Foreign exchange:
  • FX transactions are currency swaps, not money creation/destruction per se
  • Capital flows affect domestic money supply (imports → outflows; exports → inflows)
  • Impossible trinity: can't simultaneously have free capital flows + fixed exchange rate + independent monetary policy
---

Chapter 7: Conclusions

Credit theory of money wins: Historical and empirical evidence overwhelmingly supports credit theory over commodity theory. Money is a social relationship, not a natural phenomenon.

Money is a social relationship backed by the state: Its value depends on collective trust and state enforcement (legal tender, tax obligations).

Implications for reform:

Current system problems:

  • Banks create money as debt → all money comes into existence with interest obligations attached
  • Incentives push credit toward speculation, not production
  • System is inherently unstable (periodic booms/busts)
  • Democratic legitimacy problem: private banks decide the allocation of society's purchasing power
Reform options discussed:

1. Government borrowing directly from commercial banks → credit creation for public purposes; minimal reform 2. Central bank credit creation for public spending → government spends newly created central bank money (similar to MMT approaches) 3. Money-financed fiscal expenditure (Overt Monetary Financing) → government deficit financed by new central bank money, not bonds; "helicopter money" 4. Regional/local money systems → complementary currencies, credit unions, local exchange systems

Key questions for reform:

  • Who should create money?
  • How should the allocation of new credit be decided?
  • What democratic oversight should exist over money creation?
  • How do we transition without crisis?
---

Source PDF: New Economics Foundation (Creative Commons licensed). 690KB, 168 pages.
